text: Ladies and gentlemen, welcome to Boston and the opening session of the inaugural ASM Microbe.
Please welcome ASM's president, Dr. Lynn and Quest.
This is exciting.
So welcome to ASM Microbe 2016.
We've been looking forward to this moment since we announced two years ago that we would
bring ICAAC, General Meeting, and ASM's General Meeting under one roof.
I'm really thrilled that we're finally here in Boston with over 11,000 of you
to experience what I believe will be an extraordinary meeting.
Now I'd like to welcome those of you who are joining us today remotely for today's session.
So many of you have contributed your time, expertise, and feedback to get us here.
I really want to thank you for your efforts.
I particularly like to thank all of our program committee members for their hard work
as well as acknowledge the outstanding leadership of the meeting's board,
Chair, Dr. David Hooper.
It's truly an exciting time for the Microbele Sciences, and nothing signifies this more than this opening session.
Having billed gates here today underlines the importance of our work as microbiologists
and solving many of the global challenges that are the focus of the bill in Melinda Gates Foundation.
Through this foundation, Bill Gates works with his wife Melinda to expand
opportunity of the world's most disadvantaged people by collaborating with grantees and partners.
By investing in discovery research and building a global network of created minds,
Bill Gates brings people together to address the greatest global health challenges we face.
To lead what I know will be a fascinating conversation with Mr. Gates today.
We're fortunate to have Dr. Richard Besser, ABC News Chief Health and Medical Editor to interview and have a conversation with Dr. Gates with Mr. Gates.
A pediatrician by training Dr. Besser previously spent many of his years at the CDC addressing significant public health issues,
including acting, serving as acting director during the H1N1 influenza epidemic.
His background as a physician, a public health advocate, and the journalist will continue and will contribute to a rich discussion today.
This is a significant moment for ASM, and it's truly an honor to be part of it.
Please join me in welcoming Bill Gates to the stage who's going to give us a few opening remarks before his discussion with Dr. Besser.
Well, good afternoon. It's exciting to be here.
I left Boston that has dropped out of Harvard about 40 years ago.
And if you'd asked me at the time whether I'd return to talk to microbiologists and be learning about plate culture and microbiomes and all those things I would have said absolutely not.
That was not the direction I was going in.
But a number of things, the success of Microsoft, the creation of the foundation together with my wife Melinda, and a adopting global health as our biggest priority,
has meant that I've had a great opportunity to learn about health and think about the important work that all of you do.
One of the key things our foundation is prioritizing is reducing child to death.
And here there's definitely good news and bad news. The good news is that there's been a huge reduction over the last 25 years.
We've gone from about 9% of children under five dying to now 4.3%.
That is really phenomenal. That's a faster decline than ever in the history of the world.
But it's still almost 6 million children a year.
So we have a lot of work to do to take the latest in science and the resource of the world and cut that number even more dramatically.
In fact, the goal is very simple. It's global health equity.
That is a child and a poor country should have no more of a chance of dying than a child in a rich country.
And today they are more than 50 times more likely to die. So still plenty to be done.
The pie chart here is a pretty central one. It shows the causes of death for children under the age of five.
And what you can see is we've got a few things that are very clear.
Diaria, pneumonia, and malaria. And for each of those, we have a pretty clear understanding.
New drugs, new vaccines, getting the coverage of those things out there.
There's no doubt that our goal for the next 15 years, which is to cut the child to death rate in half again.
For those diseases, I think we're absolutely on track to get there.
We need resources, some new discoveries, but we have that robot map in mind.
The biggest part of this pie, you'll see there labeled the O'Neil, is children who die in the first 30 days of life.
And almost half of that's in the first seven days and almost half of that is in the first day.
There's a variety of causes of subscribe, but it's fair to say that we actually know far less about that than we need to.

And so unlike in the other cases, where the kind of tools and things we need to do are clear, in this case we actually need a lot more discovery to understand what the elements are and how we could drive that down.
Our foundation invests in all these diseases, developing countries including the adult diseases like HIV and TB.
We have great collaborations both with the private sector, with the universities, with the government institutions.
It's really been great to see how we can get a critical mass of people and rally around this cause.
One of the things we did was create all the way back at the beginning in 2000, a group to buy vaccines for the poor countries called Gabi.

Some amazing vaccines of course have been invented, but ironically those vaccines, the natural courses that they're used in rich countries for the kids who have the least chance of being exposed to the disease.
And it takes over 25 years before they get out into the poor countries where the children are most at risk.
But for two of the new miracle vaccines, specifically Rotavirus and Numa Caucus, that timeline has been eliminated.
And it's been great to see.
This chart shows the coverage of how between now and 2020, we expect to get Rotavirus into every country and literally to every child on the planet.
Likewise for Numa Caucus vaccine, you can see a very similar curve, actually even a little bit ahead of Rotavirus introduction.
And so these are the kinds of things that will be the reason we can cut those deaths in half again.
I wanted to talk just quickly about one particular cause, it's the foundation program I spend the most time on, and that's polio eradication.
This started back in 1988 with Rotary and other partners, well before we were involved.
But starting in 2003 and then in 10th, in 2007, we got very involved to make sure that this eradication would succeed.
And at the time, we had a lot of pieces in India and in Nigeria, and it looked very, very tough.
But in fact, the last few years have gone well zooming in, these are the case numbers for the last few years.
Now we're down to just two countries that have polio.
We haven't had a case for over three years in India, three and a half years and about two years in Africa.
So it's just Pakistan and Afghanistan.
The last one percent is very, very hard. You've got to get all the kids even when, there's unrest even when,
the Taliban in some cases is targeting the polio workers.
So there's no guarantee but with luck, the last case of polio should be sometime next year.
And then we wait three years to be certified and become the second disease ever to human disease to be eradicated.
We do need to do a lot more in surveillance after the Ebola epidemic.
A lot of people got together and said this is important.
Hopefully all the resources will be there but our foundation is building six sites in Africa.
We're going to get biopsied samples and really understand what's going on with diseases.
The diseases that are already there and new things that come along.
And all of the great new tools that you're inventing and working with are why that surveillance network will give us a much better understanding of what we need to do.
So the goal is global health equity.
You know, I hope all of you think about these infectious diseases and the work we still need to do because the opportunity to make in credible progress is phenomenal.
So I'm excited to be here. Thank you.
Thanks so much for those remarks. It's a real pleasure to be here at the at the ASM meeting.
I was telling you before I used to come in my days when I was at CDC and founded extremely stimulating.
We're here with over 7,000 scientists who are in the room who are working a lot of these topics.
And I have a number of questions that I want to ask you and then a number of people in the audience submitted questions.
We'll take a few of those and then come back to some of some of my questions.
So let's go ahead and get started.
You use the term catalytic philanthropy.
What is it that you mean by catalytic philanthropy?
Well, hopefully a few dollars can have a large impact.
And there's a couple of ways that that can work. One is that if you can help invent a new tool, a new vaccine, a new drug, then the effects can be super dramatic.
And the marketplace when you have poor people with diseases, it doesn't work to create an incentive there.
So government and philanthropy has to do it.
The other thing that can be catalytic is if you have a delivery system like a primary healthcare system in a country that's not working well.
That they're not training people properly or doing the supply chain ride, measuring their performance.
Going in and getting pilots getting better information there, you can raise the quality of that up.
And so a little bit of investment done properly is catalytic and then you get vaccine coverage.
And all those primary healthcare things to improve. And for us, that's often the limiting factor is that delivery system.
I want to want to ask you a question about the portfolio of the Gates Foundation.
The Gates Foundation clearly the largest player when it comes to international health as foundations go.

But according to the Institute for Health, Metrics and Evaluation, which you support, infectious diseases and childhood illnesses related to malnutrition are no longer responsible for the largest global health burden.
It's now shifting in many places to chronic disease, heart disease, diabetes, cancer, injuries, mental health.
Is the Gates Foundation driving the global health agenda in the wrong direction by not addressing these issues?
Well, what we do is we take anything that is the highest impact intervention and make sure that gets done.
And so for us today, if we can't save a life for around $1,000, it's not a good intervention.
So where's that number from?
If you do more measles campaigns, you're saving lives for $1,000.
When you introduce the new macoccus vaccine, you're actually saving lives for less than $1,000.
So as we look at our different trade-offs, it's incredible how far-dollars go.
It's almost, I think, to say, how come more resources aren't going into this?
Because in terms of health equity, we're treating the lives in these countries as being worth less than 1% of what we treat a life in the rich country.
In terms of those statistics, the years of life lost in infectious disease is still the biggest part.
Now, our success means that that will no longer be the case.
The ammonia has gone down a lot.
Diaries gone down a lot.
malaria, we're even talking about, although it's in a 30-year timeframe about making that another disease like polio that gets eradicated.
Some of the things we do, like HPV, vaccine, or anti-smoking work we do, are non-infectious disease activities.
But they meet this incredible test that the impact is very, very high.
So success would mean that we get to move on.
There are some heart disease interventions that are very cost effective nowadays.
The chemotherapy that are today very expensive.
I hope that those come down.
I hope someday that can become a priority as well.
So if the interventions are there, that are proven effective at that threshold, that could be an area that you would move into.
Or we'd help invent something that would work at that level.
Sometimes we get tiered pricing.
So then you have to just look at the cost, the delivery cost, the manufacturing cost, what that tool would be.
But we do partner on something like TB, which although it's mostly prevalent in the poor countries, it's everywhere.
So our partner would get the market for the middle income and rich countries, and we'd get a very low price for the developing countries in return for helping to fund the R&D.
I want to get to some of those topics in a little bit.
I want to start with a discussion around polio, because it clearly is such a big focus and interest of the foundation.
Looking at the 2015 investments, $463 million polio is the single largest outlay.
I want to know whether you think you are going to be able to knock it out of the Pakistan Afghanistan region.
But first I want to ask about the amount of money that's being spent.
If you ask health ministers in the countries that you're working in, in the last countries that are being affected, polio probably wouldn't be in the top 10 in terms of their health concerns.
Is it right to focus that much effort on polio when other diseases, pneumonia, rotavirus are claiming so many more lives?
In an eradication, if you succeed, then you get to zero.

Zero is a magic number because when you get to zero, then you're not only saving the medical costs of treating people and the lifelong effects in this case of paralysis, but you're also saving all the intervention costs.
And so the world today spends a lot over a billion dollars a year on polio prevention.
If we get to zero, which I'm optimistic, but I'm biased because I'm so close to it, then you don't have to spend any of that money.
So the returns are unbelievable. Probably the highest return ever in global health was smallpox eradication.
And the last year, yeah, they spent a hundred million to get rid of like 30 cases.
But then every year, since then, the lack of disease and the lack of having to even think about vaccination and its what a side effects it has, that's been a mind-blowing return.
So eradication is not to be done lightly because, per case at the end, you are spending an immense amount in order to get all those years of zero out in the future.
So if you try and fail, you hurt the credibility of the endeavor and you waste a lot of money and you miss prioritized things.
In the case of polio, what we have to do is take all the energy we put into it where we're mapping the villages, where we're training healthcare workers, where we're fixing the cold chain.
We have to make the legacy of polio the fact that routine immunization for things like measles and diarrhea, that those are far better because we were there strengthening those health systems.
Will this strategy that has proven very effective in Nigeria and India work in Pakistan, Afghanistan?
And how detrimental was it?
The efforts that were undertaken that used polio vaccinators in a way where they weren't necessarily just vaccinating for polio.
Yeah, the, what's referred to there is that the CIA did a fake vaccination campaign.
In the movie it was polio vaccine, but in real life it wasn't polio vaccine because polio vaccine were most used to use P.B. which are drops.
There was hepatitis B because they wanted to get genetic material because they would have had a syringe with some blood in it.
They could have seen the genetic lineage of the children that were in that compound.
In terms of trust of vaccinators.
It didn't help.
And a lot of people who run public health schools wrote a letter to the president and the president, subsequently, said that that tactic of a fake vaccination campaign,
because of its ill effects on the credibility of vaccination, that the US would not use that again.
I have to say it wasn't the key thing that's held us back there.
The key thing that's held us back there is that the Taliban for reasons that are hard to understand in a rational framework,
they've decided polio vaccination is a bad thing.
And so they target these vaccinators.
And the entire area they controlled was zero stand, which had about 600,000 children in it.
They didn't allow any vaccination in there.
And so in the Pakistani army went in, our case numbers, we just weren't seeing those cases.
The case numbers went up as those kids moved around the country.
We're spotted in infected others.
Now that in accessible kids is down to a pretty small amount.
But it's their opposition that explains why those are the last two countries in the world with polio.
One of the paradoxes in public health, you alluded to, as you get closer and closer to controlling public health problem,
the funding to support it tends to go away.
And it tends to come back.
That's the situation with TB and the US around the globe.
Have you had difficulty maintaining the support for polio as you're getting closer?
We're going to people and saying, okay, there's less cases and now we need to spend twice as much money.
So the global budget went from about 500 million a year to about a billion a year for starting about three years ago and through the next several years.
Now we feel good.
We got a lot of governments to come on board.
We wrote a read, goes out to its membership and still raises funds there.
But it's not an easy case to make and there was a lot of fatigue.
People had said that it would be finished in India again and again and again and again and it wasn't.
And people could go to Nigeria and if the data that the vaccine that's reporting was correct, kids would have been vaccinated 30 times.
But there was no kid that had been vaccinated 30 times.
In fact, even the population numbers were wrong.
The program was really, really had to be completely redesigned with lots of measurement and the satellite maps that showed us where people live were an absolutely critical element of it.
So, yeah, polio came close to not moving forward.
So you don't want to start one of these things lightly.
We are starting a malaria eradication and we think, you know, that's a good thing.
But you have to think hard before you declare that approach.
Well, that was my next topic, Gary.
I wanted to ask you about malaria eradication and it's one of the things the foundation is committed to and one of the approaches really, really highlights the innovation side of what you do.
And that's the use of the CRISPR technology, gene editing, to combine with gene drives to possibly put through a gene that could eradicate.
The species of mosquito that is spreading malaria.
Is that essential for the eradication of malaria or could it be done with with bednets and existing technology?
How important is new technology to accomplish this?
We need new technology.
And the two ones that would be the most impactful would be to have a vaccine.
The vaccine we have today has a modest duration.
So unless we can find some new dosing strategy, we're going to need a next generation vaccine.
And the second is this tool which would drop the enoffalling vector.
Either modify it genetically so it can't carry the parasite and we have some constructs that work that way.
Or as you said, drop the population.
And what's unusual about this is that it's a genetic construct that whether the mother of the father.
Have this then in the progeny carries it and so it propagates.
It's a gene drives.
It's a gene drives.
So it propagates very effectively into the into the population.
We've done a lot of modeling of that.
It's not ready for prime time.
There's still some scientific work going on.
And then it'll be a kind of a novel regulatory process because there hasn't been something like this in the past.
But yes, I think particularly in the very fine prevalence areas, which are parts of Africa,
it's just so difficult to stop a rebound that dropping the population numbers are going to be part of the eradication.
In follow-up to that.
A question that really relates to when technology is ready for use.
The national academies of science, engineering and medicine, they released the statement,
cautioning that there wasn't enough evidence yet to safely release genetically modified mosquitoes into the wild.
And question is, who gets to decide when there's enough evidence to release something like this that may not be able to be reversed?
Well, the mosquitoes won't get to vote on this one.
It's sovereign, and the end of the day, it's sovereign nations that decide what the trade-offs are.
You know, we see that in terms of agricultural technology, medical technology.
There is a large part of a people look to the United States because our regulatory structures are being built.
Because our regulatory structures are viewed as being the highest quality.
Now that sometimes can mean that if you have something where the benefits to the U.S. are very, very low,
and so it thinks of the risk reward ratios as not being good enough, it may for some individual country be something where maybe they should think of it differently.
But in most cases, people want either Europe or the United States to blast new medical technology.
And so I think likely one of those top regulators will need to weigh in on this gene drive.
It's also possible that a country could decide on its own, but I don't think that's the probable case.
In that situation, given that mosquitoes don't respect borders.
You could have a situation where one country says, yeah, we want to give this a trial, and the border in country says, we don't think it's safe yet.
Do we have the global framework in place to be able to handle some of the technologies that you all are working on?
Well, we always have problems that are global in nature.
Climate change is like that.
Somebody could decide to do geoengineering or not participate in CO2 reduction.
Epidemics are like that.
Wars are like that.
And the coordination of how you get consensus on those things, it is very, very difficult, particularly if your lack of surveillance for say epidemic disease.
Put your neighbor risk.
How do they convince you that you ought to do that?
On this mosquito thing, I just, I don't think that many problems are going to come up.
That is the pro mosquito lobby.
I don't think it's going to come up with huge reasons.
We ought to go through, understand everything about this, but my guess is that this tool will find use.
That will be a big reason we will take the happening year kids who die from malaria and drop that dramatically.
You know, it's here in the, in the US, there's a lot of concern about the Zika spread through the Caribbean and South America.
And one of the potential solutions there is, again, a genetically modified mosquito.
And we are involved in this being applied in parts of Brazil and dramatic reductions in mosquitoes that could carry dengue fever or Zika.
In Florida, where there's been approval for testing, the community has come out and said, no, we don't think that this is a way to go forward.
The question I have has to do somewhat with, with another part of your portfolio, which is education.
And the role of scientific literacy to be able to move forward with some of the things that you want to do.
Is that an area that, I didn't notice scientific literacy in your portfolio, but I do see literacy.
Yeah, our education program and our global health program, there really isn't a synergistic element where we teach people about vaccines and they, you know,
they're super enlightened in terms of how they think about these things.
The general view of some of these vector interventions changes once a population is at risk.
So it'll be interesting to see like in that Florida case, as Zika's actually coming in, you know, how people think about that.
We're not involved in the oxy-textural mail, which is the one that there was some initial reaction to the thing we're involved in, mostly that actually could get rolled out.
And some locations is a will-bocca infection of the 80s mosquito.
And that causes the amount of the number mosquitoes that carry a number of viruses, including dengue and Zika, to go down quite dramatically.
We, over the last 12 years, have done a lot of community trials.

First in Australia, then in Indonesia, then in the Philippines, and actually have had, because we've done a good job of community education, actually, our partners who actually are doing the work handled that very well.
It's a very positive reception to that.
So all of these things, you can't just jump into a community without explaining the risk rewards being honest, taking time, letting people hear both sides of the arguments.
And Zika's come across, so quickly, that those discussions really are just to start.
And last March, you were very vocal.

You published an article in New England Journal, as well as the New York Times op-ed that was really, it was a wake-up call to the global health community about response to epidemics and preparedness for epidemics.
And you wrote about the importance of investing in health systems, disease surveillance, and other aspects of public health infrastructure.
Clearly, there's less interest in by donors and governments in funding infrastructure than there is in funding efforts around a particular disease.
Yet infrastructure is essential for being able to detect epidemics and respond to new problems.
When I look at the Gates Foundation portfolio, it again is very disease-specific.
Why does the Foundation not invest more directly in lab scientists and epidemiologists in health communicators in a general fashion rather than a disease-specific fashion?
Well, we're the biggest external funder for primary health care systems.
And that is really at the heart of the quality of delivery.
And you'd be surprised.
There are countries, even at very low levels of wealth, who run extremely good primary health care systems, Rwanda, Ethiopia, Sri Lanka, Senegal.
They've gone out of a cadre of people who make sure the supply chain, the training, the staffing that they're doing a very good job there.
And that makes a huge difference in those health outcomes.
And so that's the primary delivery.
It's not doctors in our case.
Most of the people in these countries will never meet a doctor during their entire lifetime.
It's that clinic that's out there in the rural areas, serving people.
And understanding how we can outbought without.
That's increasing importance.
It's been the fastest growing program we have is what we call integrated delivery to help take the best practices from the countries I mentioned and try to get the other countries up to that level.
Let me just push on that a little bit.

The clinical care is one side, but the public health system, the people who are going to look across your clinics and identify, wow, they're seeing an increase in pneumonia across this region of the country.
And the laboratories that will then be able to look at that and respond to that.
During my time at CDC and currently it's been very hard to get government to say that's an important thing to do and an important thing to fund.
And recently Congress has said, take the money that was going to build those systems in Africa after Ebola and use it on Zika.
Yeah, the question should an outside country help fund that sort of CDC equivalent in these African countries is very interesting.
And the U.S. has been not as generous as either you or I would like, but we have been the most generous of any country.

And the global health security agenda, the money that was budgeted after Ebola was going to actually pick a number of African countries and have CDC go into a very important job there, which they do an absolutely fantastic job of that.
And as you said, that money may be deverted, it's still kind of confusing right now that money might come back.
That is it's a mistake not to fund those things.
We tend to specialize in the primary health care clinic, which is what we know.
We do less on the public health system broadly.

One concept that we have been working with the CDC on is that we saw during the Ebola outbreak, the most effective response was taking polio people who understood public communication and emergency operation centers and case tracking.
Because polio interrupting it for three or four months wasn't a problem.
We could take that capacity.
And in fact, Nigeria was the polio people there who took the six cases and made sure it wasn't 6,600,000.
That would have been absolutely horrific.
So this time as we do malaria, we're going to train the people involved in these malaria eradication efforts to be available for their outbreak.
So we're going to formalize this notion that they're the ones who are actually practicing.
They're not somebody sitting somewhere who hasn't actually done it.
And they will be able to move to wherever in the world there is a problem.
It's been hard to get the capacity.
People's willingness to fund armies that kind of sit around waiting for wars.
Because there's been so many wars that's a paradigm that's well understood, they're willing to fund that.
But then when it comes to the epidemic, which actually in terms of killing a lot of people, I would rate most people who are just a great at risk.
They haven't been as generous on that.
So we need to educate people and get more global capacity and local capacity.
I want to ask a little bit about shared responsibility and the role of foundations.
Gates foundation is the largest foundation working on global health.
And do you have any concerns that the size of your footprint globally is letting governments off the hook in terms of their responsibility?
Well certainly if I was seeing a country rich, melancholy, come or low income that was spending less on these issues, that would be a very bad sign.
Particularly because we were there.
Fortunately, there is more money going into these things.
And understand, although our money by foundation standards is very large, we're 5 billion a year, the total overseas aid is 130 billion a year.
The US government has one program, the AIDS Relief Program, which is a miraculous wonderful program that's 6 billion a year.
That is that program alone is bigger than everything the foundation does in the US and overseas.
So we are a visible player in this and that we can praise the stuff that governments are doing.
We can spend our money in a little bit more flexible way than other other people can.
But compared to say the NIH budget, the farm R&D budgets, these aid budgets, we're not a very big thing.
But we feel like we've been a catalytic factor that more money is going into this from the rich countries, more from other philanthropists.
Global health in general, since 2000, has seen a gigantic increase, including the global lines for vaccines, the global fund.
Now we're worried that maybe that's going to plateau, but since 2000, the global health stories is a pretty amazing story, including getting more resources.
I have a question about global institutions, in particular, the world health organization, following the Ebola crisis.
There was widespread consensus that the WHO had failed. They had not responded in appropriate fashion.
And I want to get your perspective, is that as an organization, as an institution obsolete?
Do we need a different kind of global institution?
And if we do, would it look like the WHO, but better funded?

Well, it's pretty stunning that even the modest things that all these post-it-bola panels of the green on, in terms of better staffing and an emergency fund, things like that, even those things people aren't coming forward for the money for WHO.
There's a whole ton of stuff WHO, just isn't funded to do. WHO has no doctors, they have no planes.
They have no innate capacity to go out and do things.

When the Ebola crisis got very bad and it looked like the airlines might no longer find to those companies because they were worried about their employees getting infected, it actually took a commitment by the US military to go in.
Now fortunately, the airlines didn't stop there, turned out the nature of the disease was such that most people were bedridden by the time they were infectious.
But we do have this huge gap between what people think the WHO is funded to do and what they're funded to do.
Now even part of what they're funded to do, they didn't in perfect job.
And I'll give the director general a lot of credit for being willing to say, hey, the WHO could have done better.
She could have chosen to ascribe that to some of her sub-offices.
She didn't choose to do that. She took the blame for the organization as the whole and participated in a discussion of how to do those things better.
But there's a lot that is unfunded that neither WHO or domestic governments are funded to do.
The US at least has barra that's there to think about these things.
There is no barra equivalent in any other government in the world.

So that leads to my next question. I'm going to ask one more question and then go to the questions we have in the audience that people submitted ahead of time and then come back to some more questions.
I could ask questions for hours.
So this question, I was recently at the school world forum meeting in England.
And someone in the audience said that the first approach to tackling global health problems should always be a free market solution.

However, many of the diseases that impact parts of the world, there in places where markets fail, where no pharmaceutical company that is truly looking at their bottom line in fiduciary responsibility would develop a new drug.
How has the Gates Foundation approach that problem?
Well, many of the diseases we work on are present in the rich world, which you could say that's unfortunate or fortunate.
HIV is is paradigmatic.
So the drug companies have done a phenomenal job creating new HIV drugs.
And the anti-retroviral field before that had very few great products.
And now the discovery rate, different than the overall discovery rate, the discovery rate of new HIV medicines has been really strong.
And a very extreme form of tiered pricing, where they're able to pay for their R&D based on the U.S. and other rich countries buying those medicines.
And then making them available literally at cost and letting the generic manufacturers, including those in India, make them as cheap as they possibly can.
That's been a perfect model.
And so far, it's been insented to do their brilliant work and the availability and poor countries of those drugs has been really great.
In fact, the greatest cost now is not the drugs.
That's down to like 50 cents a day.
It's actually the personnel costs.
And so we're very involved in trying to get those numbers down.
TB, thank goodness, it's still in the rich world so that people can invent new drugs and use this tiered price and approach.
The place where you don't get that is in the so-called neglected diseases in malaria, where because prophylactic drugs can solve the problem, the rich countries really don't care about it.
And that's where philanthropy or government aid absolutely are the only way to get the R&D spending.
Can you say a little something about epidemic meningitis in Africa?
Yeah, that was a pretty exciting thing where there had been expensive vaccines.
Ironically, vaccines are often used where the risk is lost, but we had great meningitis vaccines.
They were too expensive to be used in the so-called meningitis belt in Africa, which just had meningitis A.
And so we worked with the manufacturer, in this case was serum in India.
We worked with the regulator, was actually the Canadian regulator, and got a 35 cent vaccine approved.
And that's cheap enough that we've been able to go throughout the men and child's belt and get people of all ages who showed up for this vaccine and drugs.
As soon as they heard it was available because the kind of panic you get when the waves of disease would come through.
We're pretty huge.
People know about the disease. And so now we're almost done now, treating all the people in that area and infants.
It's now approved. They will get it.
And so the number of cases is been near and zero in an area where from some years it was very, very dramatic.
So it's a great example of for 30 cents a dose.
Which is funded by my God, the for the countries that can't even fund that.
If we have the microphone, let's go to Debbie Gough in the audience who has a question for Bill Gates.
My question is from your perspective, as the wealthy United States is not winning the battle against super bugs.
What do you think should be next in the global armamentarium to fight super bugs?
Well, evolution is very clever. It created us.
And in the case of microbes, the life cycles are pretty short.
We have horse-on transfer. So in a way, it's not surprising that this is something where it's a constant battle you have to go after.
Like, for most diseases, the number of people who die of antichromatic home resistance is a hundred times larger in the poor countries than it is in the United States.
It's sad that it's in the United States, but because it's in the United States, lots of for-profit incentives there to replenish the pipeline for antibiotics.
And the latest science in terms of how do you culture, bacteria, how do you look at the DNA of bacteria and see, even if you don't know how to turn on the expression of the antibiotic.
If you can just look at the DNA, you can find it there, and that can give you a sense of new leads for antibiotics.
So the candidates that can become available, science will be able to come up with a lot of those.
In the US, there'll be strong incentives, I.E. High pricing.
I want to push back on that.
It's been more than 20 years since there's been a new class of antibiotics.
And, you know, with all of the mergers of pharmaceutical companies, there's less R&D that's taking place.
And as a public health community, what we're asking these companies is we want you to make a new antibiotic, and we don't want anyone to use it.
Because as soon as they start using it, we're going to see resistance rise.
And so you have that market failure.
Again, where there are ND is going for drugs for diabetes and hypertension and sexual dysfunction.
And no one is making drugs for super bugs.
You've got to make sure that the price and opportunity for them of a drug that's only going to be used in very small volume, that it's very good.
And we have mechanisms. The orphan drug legislation, you can argue, should have been slightly more generous or slightly less generous.
But that's another bright spot in an otherwise fairly bleak picture in terms of new discoveries per year.
The orphan diseases, because the appropriate reward system was put in place, there's lots of good things that have gone on there.
And so it is true for future drugs where you're going to restrict usage, and we need to do that for several of them.
You need to make sure that there's a special incentive that makes clear for people that either they'll get a minimum amount of usage or some other way.
And I'll see value. But I'm saying that the scientific opportunity to great new antibiotics is there.
That is evolution created a lot more chemicals than we've then we've discovered like thousands of times more than we've discovered.
And so it's a business model problem.
And there's a lot of good discussion about this. There's reasons why trials have been hard to do again.
You know, the healthy versus non-healthy that the trial regimes are a little typical.
And the people have not felt like if they price an antibiotic high that they would be sure that they'd get some reward for that.
So that's, you know, the annual report and other things are talking about this.
The ritual antibiotic thing, there are solutions coming. It's not going to be some out of control.
Well, in new drug or a new approach to killing microbes.
Well, there are various, mostly I think it'll be a new drug because that the system is set up.
There are things like bacteriophage approaches that because the drugs were so cheap that the Russians and others had played around with that may now come back to the floor.
Because there's a non-met need.
All right, let's turn to our next question from the audience, can I a name of?
Sir, as a philanthropist, investing in countless research programs all around the world.

What effort has your foundation made in facilitating the absorption of young and upcoming scientists into the various research institutes and establishments in which you sponsor in scientific research?
Yeah, there's a huge challenge, which is that most of the spending we do to create a new vaccine or a drug is actually spent on the trials and most of that is spent in the affected countries.
And so getting the capacity of the scientists in, I'll just say, in Africa a lot of this work is in Asia as well.
Helping this new generation of scientists come along has been a key part of that.

We do that in our agricultural work, to encourage new young scientists, we do it in our family health work, and we do it in the scientific realm where we need to have institutions in Africa that are very good.
Whether it's doing the work independently or simply being a partner for the trials, that's been very important.
And South Africa is where we've seen the greatest capacity of the rest of the continent.
There is this huge vacuum and the only thing that will fill that in is getting the young scientists, those opportunities.
Getting young scientists that often come to the US and then making sure there's an opportunity for them that it's attracted them to go back to Africa.
There's often a fair bit of a tradition there, but we're working on that.
So my question is, how can we better update regulators to expedite adoption of transformative microbiome methods that improve human health?
How are you how impaired are we by the current regulatory systems?
Well, there's certainly times we sit and say, wow, we have this miracle new thing and how come it's taking so long how come our trials so big.
And so we're all, I'd say the regulatory system is pretty good.
Now it's needed to make special accommodation for HIV.
It did make special accommodation to get those drugs out because there was a huge crisis there.
In terms of the microbiome, you know, maybe there's something that I'm not aware of that we need to change there.
But so far, the work has really been at the research level to understand in our case under nutrition and for other cases, over nutrition.
And one of the approaches, a company that I met last week, is actually taking an engineered E.
We call it bacteria and you eat that as a probiotic.
The one they're starting out, it doesn't colonize.
It just passes through your stomach.
So you have to re-dose yourself every few days.
But it could, if you have certain metabolic diseases, do that metabolism in these modified E.
And in our case, we're under nutrition.
It's generating certain things like butorate that are beneficial to the development of the microbiome.
It looks to me like the regulatory approach is going to be reasonable.
They're going to ask the right questions and over the next four or five years, some of those tools will get out there.
We're currently in the midst of the big political season.
And the primary is over in the general election is coming.
And during the primary season, I don't think a single candidate mentioned anything having to do with global health.
And as people now look towards priorities, priorities for our country, how do you make the case to Americans?
The investing in global health is a good thing to do when there are so many health problems here in the United States.
Well I'd say the history on this for the last 16 years is very positive.
That is both the Bush and the administration, Obama administration, made huge commitments to global health programs.
In the case of the Bush administration, the very creation of the President's emergency plan for AIDS relief, Pet Far, and the President's military initiative came in that administration.
I was at an HIV conference and I mentioned President Bush and I didn't, you know, I said he's done great work and I got kind of a negative response.
I was a little surprised about that because really he, his personal leadership was fairly keen getting something dramatic done there, although I will say it's been totally bipartisan since then.
Likewise, President Obama's spoken out on these issues.
He's gotten some additional funds, although not many budget categories gone up.
We got to get an increase in terms of the polio funding that this administration helped us.
We had to go up on the hill with Rotorion, make the case.
And it wasn't a huge amount of money, but it allowed us to go to the other donors and show that the U.S. was doing its part.
And so the U.S. has been a responsible player in global health that directs its R&D, the way it directs its foreign aid, the way it takes CDC expertise.
You know, the number of people in CDC who went out and spent their time in the Ebola-fectory countries, it's an amazing story that, you know, they don't expect that credit because it's what they do.
But, you know, what an amazing organization. And believe me, there is the next best equivalent to CDC is not even close.
In terms of that, that depth of capability and ability to go out and help on a global basis.
So I'm hopeful that we'll retain this positive record that we've had.
You know, some candidates haven't shown a full understanding of vaccines and the benefits they provide.
And some, you know, haven't shown an embrace of the world that large in a way that might be beneficial.
But we have to hope that the great tradition that the U.S. should be proud of it the last 60 years that that continues.
All right. Well, I want to shift gears and ask you some questions about the foundation.
So in the room here, there's 7,000 plus scientists from around the world.
They come from a very different culture than Microsoft.
You've spent many years now working in the foundation.
And can you talk a little bit about the difference between the cultures?
Is it the same model that you're applying to the foundation that you applied to Microsoft?
And or if you had to shift your way of doing business?
Well, there are some things that are absolutely identical.
You want people are very analytical, very engaged, very open minded, want to learn new things.
Who embrace science, who understand that science is a 10 to 15 year endeavor and you have to pursue multiple approaches.
There are some things that are different. The bottom line at Microsoft was the volume that software we sold and the profitability that generated.
The bottom line for the foundation is the under five mortality rate, the nutrition, the eradication of diseases, access to reproductive health tools.
And so our metrics are different.
And the skill sets, they overlap. The analytical skills are the same.
But the foundation has to be far better at working in poor countries, countries where the government often has very limited capacity.
You know, understanding how you avoid any misuse of the funds in your programs.
We have to be better at working with governments in the rich world, making sure that their aid money is complimentary to what we're doing,
making sure that the R&D funding gets there. And so it's interesting. I'm spending a lot more time with governments in this role,
but foundation role than I ever did in the Microsoft role.
And I'm really pleased the willingness to talk and collaborate with the foundation is really quite incredible.
The mix of skills, scientists, people have worked in the field.
The difficulty of delivering these products means that you've got to have people who've seen field delivery weigh in on that product profile.
So there's maybe six or seven deep types of skills that have to come together for us to have the impact we want to have.
Which has been more rewarding?
Starting and running Microsoft or starting and running the Gates Foundation.
Well, I think if I tried to do this foundation work in my 20s and 30s, I would have found it incredibly frustrating.
You know, in my 20s and 30s, I wrote the code. I stayed in all night. I wrote the code. I didn't go home until it worked.
Once I Britain the code, I could ship it. I didn't have to call the FDA.
And people could tell me if it was wrong, I could update my code.
You know, just that kind of hands-on engineering, quick cycle.
You know, the creation of the personal computer of the software industry.
You know, I was just all in just fanatically focused on that one thing.
Now I'm older, you know, I can fund a lot of programs.
You know, so I'm not calling a group on say the HIV group.
You know, I don't call them on Monday and say, how's your vaccine? We're going and then call them on Tuesday and say, well, have you made any progress?
You know, we have enough stuff that I only say that to them once every couple of months.
And say, hey, have you done two months of progress in the last two months?
So the kind of patients required these kind of complex relationships.
I think I find those more rewarding. I'm better at those things at this age.
So the Microsoft stuff was perfect for my young career and the foundation stuff is perfect for this phase of my life.
And what are you seeing? Looking for what do you see as the biggest global health challenges that people here will be facing?
Well, absent a epidemic that would come in surprises, whether it's flu or some other thing.
And the big threat there is some human-to-human respiratory transmitted thing, which could either be naturally emerging or intentionally created.
Put that aside. If I had one wand to solve a medical problem, it would be to solve nutrition.
The impact in these countries, particularly in Africa, of kids who don't grow up and develop physically and mentally is really unbelievable.
That's a greater opposition on African, its ability to develop and support itself than any other thing.
Now right after that, you'd have HIV, malaria, and TB.
And I'm hopeful that those four wishes, all of those in the next 20 years, we will have tools to solve nutrition and vaccines or other approaches that can solve those diseases.
So I'm hopeful that the foundation will see the end of most of the problems that we've made a priority.
Well, as a closing question, you have a captive audience here of some of the world's leading scientists when it comes to infectious diseases.
What would you like to leave them with tonight?
Well, there's such a need to solve these mysteries.
And the mysteries of nutrition are really unbelievable, whether it's under nutrition or over nutrition.
The need to diagnose things like to see microbial resistance ideally at the point of care, to even understanding does a patient have a bacterial or viral disease.
In Africa, if you come in with a fever, you're given malaria drugs.
Well, it's possible that you need antibiotics or some other intervention.
But we're masking by not being able to tell what's going on.

So the opportunity to understand the microbiome, the opportunity to have really deep diagnosis, kind of skills represented here and the innovation in these areas will be absolutely critical to our treatment, our goals.
Well, it's been a delight speaking with you on behalf of ASM.
I want to thank you for coming and opening the meeting.
And that concludes the opening session and you are all invited to go across the street to the opening reception.
Thank you very much.
Thanks very much.
Yeah, that was fun.
If you are attending the opening reception, please exit the session and proceed to the front of the building towards the registration area.
Exit the center on the registration level and cross the street to the World Trade Center.
Look for staff holding signs with the ASM micro-blogo for directions to the reception.
